Retention Risk & Customer Value

Author

Group 7

1. Introduction

1.1. Project Background

Fintech companies operate in highly competitive digital markets where customer engagement and retention are critical for long-term sustainability. Unlike traditional banks, fintech platforms rely heavily on digital interactions between users and financial services. Understanding how customers interact with these platforms is essential for identifying profitable customer segments and maintaining long-term customer relationships.

1.2. Why Customer Retention Matters?

Any business requires significant marketing and operational costs when it wants to attract new customers. Retaining existing customers proves to be more cost-effective and provide more value for extended periods.

Customers who remain active on a fintech platform tend to perform more financial transactions, adopt multiple financial products, and contribute higher customer lifetime value (CLV). In contrast, customers who show declining engagement or dissatisfaction may be more likely to discontinue their use of the platform.

Identifying customers with high churn risk allows fintech companies to implement specific strategies for retaining those customers. Organizations also need to track which customers produce the most value because this information allows them to distribute resources toward their most profitable customer groups.

1.3. Module Objective

The project objective is to develop a visual analytics application using R and Shiny for the interactive exploration of fintech customer data.

This tools would allow users to dynamically filter data, explore patterns across multiple dimensions, and find relationships between behavioral indicators and financial outcomes.

It is intended to help analysts compare churn risk across customer groups, examine engagement and profitability, and identify customers at risk of leaving the platform.

1.4. Methodology

This module adopts an Exploratory Modelling methodology. It focuses on exploring relationships between customer demographics, engagement behaviors, and financial performance indicators, with particular emphasis on predicting Customer Lifetime Value (CLV).

The analytical workflow begins with data preparation and transformation, where relevant variables are cleaned, standardized, and organized into meaningful groups such as demographic attributes, transactional behavior, engagement indicators, and financial outcomes. This step ensures that the dataset is suitable for both visual exploration and predictive modelling.

Following data preparation, exploratory modelling techniques are applied to investigate patterns and relationships between variables. Initial visualizations are used to explore churn probability across customer segments, allowing the identification of groups that may be at higher risk of leaving the platform. While churn exploration provides valuable contextual insights, the primary analytical focus of this module is the development of a predictive model for Customer Lifetime Value (CLV).

A machine learning predictive modelling approach is implemented to estimate CLV using behavioral, and transactional variables. This model supports the identification of key drivers of customer value and enables comparison between predicted and observed CLV values. The results are visualized through interactive charts that allow users to evaluate model performance and explore how customer characteristics influence financial value.

The final stage of the methodology integrates these analytical components into a dynamic Shiny dashboard, enabling users to interactively filter data by demographic variables such as gender, income, occupation, and age group. This interactive design supports progressive exploration, allowing users to examine churn patterns, engagement behaviors, and predicted customer value across different segments.

Overall, this methodology supports data driven decision making by combining exploratory visual analysis, churn exploration, and predictive modelling of customer lifetime value, providing insights into customer profitability and retention risk.

2. Data & Environment Preparation Process

2.1. Evaluation of R Packages

For the development of this module and the visual analytics components, several R packages are required to support data manipulation, visualization, and formatting of analytical outputs.

The main packages used in this project include:

Package Use
tidyverse Used to support the overall data science workflow, including data transformation, cleaning, and preparation of the fintech dataset.
ggplot2 Creates the core visualizations in this module, including boxplots, bar charts, scatterplots, and geographic maps.
dplyr Manipulates and summarizes the dataset, groups customers by segment or location and calculates metrics like average churn probability and average CLV.
scales Improve visualization readability by formatting large financial values, percentages, and axis labels.
plotly Adds interactivity to selected visualizations, enabling users to hover over elements.
randomForest Develop an exploratory machine learning model that predicts CLV by capturing nonlinear relationships and interactions between variables.

All these packages are hosted and maintained on CRAN, ensuring compatibility with the R environment and allowing them to be installed easily.

To verify that these packages are available in CRAN, the following command can be used in R:

packages <- c("tidyverse","readxl","ggplot2","dplyr","knitr","scales","plotly","randomForest")

sapply(packages, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)
   tidyverse       readxl      ggplot2        dplyr        knitr       scales 
        TRUE         TRUE         TRUE         TRUE         TRUE         TRUE 
      plotly randomForest 
        TRUE         TRUE

The code chunk below uses p_load() of pacman package to check if the packages are installed in the computer. If they are, then they will be launched into R.

pacman::p_load("tidyverse","readxl","ggplot2","dplyr","knitr","scales","plotly","randomForest")

2.2. Raw Dataset

The analysis uses the COFINFAD (Colombian Fintech Financial Analytics) dataset, which contains detailed information about fintech customers. The dataset includes demographic information, financial product usage, transaction activity, customer satisfaction indicators, and predicted churn probabilities.

The dataset contains approximately 48,000 customer records and more than 50 variables, providing a comprehensive view of customer behavior within a fintech platform.

fintech <- read_xlsx("customer_data.xlsx")

2.3. Variable Grouping

Although the dataset contains a wide range of variables, this module primarily focuses on variables related to:

Customer Value Indicators

These variables measure the long-term financial contribution of each customer.

customer_lifetime_value
clv_segment
customer_segment

Retention Risk Indicators

The indicators capture signals related to potential customer churn.

churn_probability
satisfaction_score
nps_score

Transaction & Engagement Behavior Indicators

These metrics indicate how actively a customer is and their engagement with the fintech platform.

monthly_transaction_count
average_transaction_value
total_transaction_volume
transaction_frequency
active_products

Geographic Variables

These fields allow spatial analysis of CLV and geographic patterns in fintech adoption.

city
department
latitude
longitude

Demographic Variables

These information help us identify the customers background and how different groups behave within fintech services.

gender
income_bracket
occupation
age

2.4. Data Cleaning

Data preparation involved various steps to ensure the dataset was suitable for analysis:

2.4.1. Verify data types for numerical and categorical variables

data_types <- data.frame(
  Variable = names(fintech),
  Data_Type = sapply(fintech, function(x) paste(class(x), collapse = ", "))
)

kable(data_types, caption = "Summary of Variable Data Types")
Summary of Variable Data Types
Variable Data_Type
customer_id customer_id numeric
age age numeric
gender gender character
city city character
department department character
latitude latitude numeric
longitude longitude numeric
income_bracket income_bracket character
occupation occupation character
education_level education_level character
marital_status marital_status character
household_size household_size numeric
acquisition_channel acquisition_channel character
customer_segment customer_segment character
savings_account savings_account logical
credit_card credit_card logical
personal_loan personal_loan logical
investment_account investment_account logical
insurance_product insurance_product logical

app_logins_frequency app_logins_frequency numeric

credit_utilization_ratio credit_utilization_ratio numeric
international_transactions international_transactions numeric
failed_transactions failed_transactions numeric
tx_count tx_count numeric
avg_tx_value avg_tx_value numeric

first_tx first_tx POSIXct, POSIXt
last_tx last_tx POSIXct, POSIXt
base_satisfaction base_satisfaction numeric
tx_satisfaction tx_satisfaction numeric

nps_score nps_score numeric
last_survey_date last_survey_date POSIXct, POSIXt
support_tickets_count support_tickets_count numeric
resolved_tickets_ratio resolved_tickets_ratio numeric
app_store_rating app_store_rating numeric
feedback_sentiment feedback_sentiment character
feature_requests feature_requests character
complaint_topics complaint_topics character
clv_segment clv_segment character
monthly_transaction_count monthly_transaction_count numeric
average_transaction_value average_transaction_value numeric
total_transaction_volume total_transaction_volume numeric
transaction_frequency transaction_frequency numeric
last_transaction_date last_transaction_date POSIXct, POSIXt
preferred_transaction_type preferred_transaction_type character
first_transaction_date first_transaction_date POSIXct, POSIXt
weekend_transaction_ratio weekend_transaction_ratio numeric
avg_daily_transactions avg_daily_transactions numeric

churn_probability churn_probability numeric

2.4.2. Check for duplicated customers

duplicate_summary <- fintech %>%
  summarise(
    Duplicate_Customers = sum(duplicated(customer_id))
  )

kable(
  duplicate_summary,
  caption = "Number of Duplicate Customer IDs"
)
Number of Duplicate Customer IDs
Duplicate_Customers
0

2.4.3. Identify any missing values in key analytical variables

missing_summary <- fintech %>%
  summarise(
    Customer_Lifetime_Value = sum(is.na(customer_lifetime_value)),
    Churn_Probability = sum(is.na(churn_probability)),
    Transaction_Frequency = sum(is.na(transaction_frequency))
  ) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = everything(),
    names_to = "Variable",
    values_to = "Missing_Values"
  )

kable(
  missing_summary,
  caption = "Summary of Missing Values in Key Analytical Variables"
)
Summary of Missing Values in Key Analytical Variables
Variable Missing_Values
Customer_Lifetime_Value 0
Churn_Probability 0
Transaction_Frequency 0

2.4.4. Remove incomplete observations where necessary

fintech_clean <- fintech %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(customer_lifetime_value),
    !is.na(churn_probability),
    !is.na(transaction_frequency)
  )
nrow(fintech) - nrow(fintech_clean)
[1] 0

2.4.5. Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) values were re scaled from absolute currency values to millions of Colombian Pesos.

fintech_clean <- fintech_clean %>%
  mutate(clv_millions = customer_lifetime_value / 1000000)

2.4.6. Age was grouped into age buckets.

fintech_clean <- fintech_clean %>%
  mutate(
    age_bucket = cut(
      age,
      breaks = c(18, 25, 35, 45, 55, 65, Inf),
      labels = c("18–25", "26–35", "36–45", "46–55", "56–65", "65+"),
      right = TRUE
    )
  )

3. Exploratory Analysis and Visualizations

The visualizations selected for this module are designed to explore the relationships between Customer Lifetime Value, churn probability, and customer engagement behavior as a foundation for predictive modelling.

The primary objective is to identify meaningful predictors and behavioral patterns that can inform the development of a robust predictive CLV model.

3.1. CLV Distribution by Customer Segment

With this visualization we aim to explore how Customer Lifetime Value varies across different customer segments to identify patterns in customer profitability. By examining the distribution of CLV using a logarithmic scale (CLV values are highly skewed), the analysis highlights differences in financial contribution between segments.

ggplot(fintech_clean, 
       aes(x = customer_segment, 
           y = clv_millions,
           fill = customer_segment)) +
  
  geom_boxplot(
    alpha = 0.8,
    outlier.alpha = 0.3,
    width = 0.6
  ) +
  
  # Add mean points
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean,
    geom = "point",
    shape = 18,
    size = 3,
    color = "darkblue"
  ) +
  
  scale_fill_brewer(
    palette = "Blues",
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  
  scale_y_log10(
    labels = comma
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Customer Lifetime Value by Segment",
    subtitle = "Distribution shown on logarithmic scale",
    x = "Customer Segment",
    y = "Customer Lifetime Value (Millions, Log Scale)"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
Warning in scale_y_log10(labels = comma): log-10 transformation introduced infinite values.
log-10 transformation introduced infinite values.
Warning: Removed 1 row containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).
Warning: Removed 1 row containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_summary()`).

From this graph, it can be observed that the distribution of CLV varies significantly across customer segments. “Power” and “Regular” customers exhibit substantially higher median CLV values compared to “Occasional” and “Inactive” customers, indicating that higher levels of engagement are strongly associated with greater long-term financial contribution.

These findings highlight the importance of prioritizing higher engagement segments in customer retention strategies and support the development of predictive models aimed at identifying customers with strong value potential.

3.2. Churn Probability by Segment

We want to explore differences in churn probability across customer segments. By comparing segments, the analysis provides insight into which groups may require targeted retention strategies.

churn_by_segment <- fintech_clean %>%
  group_by(customer_segment) %>%
  summarise(avg_churn = mean(churn_probability, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(desc(avg_churn)) %>%
  mutate(customer_segment = factor(customer_segment, levels = customer_segment))

ggplot(churn_by_segment, aes(x = customer_segment, y = avg_churn, fill = avg_churn)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = label_percent(accuracy = 0.1)(avg_churn)),
    vjust = -0.4,
    size = 4,
    fontface = "bold",
    color = "#08306b"
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low = "#c6dbef",
    high = "#08306b",
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = label_percent(),
    breaks = seq(0.30, 0.35, 0.01),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.08))
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0.30, 0.35)) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Churn Probability by Customer Segment",
    subtitle = "Segments ranked from highest to lowest churn risk",
    x = "Customer Segment",
    y = "Average Churn Probability"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  )

The results indicate that churn probability is relatively similar across segments, with only minor variations.

This suggests that churn risk alone may not strongly differentiate segments, highlighting the importance of combining churn indicators with behavioral and financial variables when developing the predictive CLV model.

3.3. Engagement Behavior vs Customer Value

Understanding the relationship between transaction frequency and Customer Lifetime Value is essential for identifying behavioral indicators that may serve as strong predictors in the development of the predictive model.

x_limit <- quantile(fintech_clean$transaction_frequency, 0.95)
y_limit <- quantile(fintech_clean$clv_millions, 0.95)

ggplot(fintech_clean, 
       aes(x = transaction_frequency, 
           y = clv_millions,
           color = customer_segment)) +
  
  geom_point(
    alpha = 0.4,
    size = 2
  ) +
  
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    se = FALSE,
    linewidth = 1,
    alpha = 0.6
  ) +
  
  scale_color_brewer(
    palette = "Blues"
  ) +
  
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = comma
  ) +
  
  coord_cartesian(
    xlim = c(0, x_limit),
    ylim = c(0, y_limit)
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Transaction Frequency vs Customer Lifetime Value",
    subtitle = "Relationship between customer engagement and financial value",
    x = "Transaction Frequency",
    y = "Customer Lifetime Value (Millions)",
    color = "Customer Segment"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40"),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

The result of this scatter plot suggest that while higher transaction frequency is generally associated with higher customer value, the relationship is not strictly linear, as significant variability in CLV exists across all frequency levels. Additional behavioral and demographic variables will be needed to improve the accuracy and robustness of the predictive CLV model.

3.4. Geographic Distribution of Customer Value

By mapping CLV by city we can identify locations associated with higher value customers and highlight geographic areas that may contribute disproportionately to overall revenue.

In order to visualize the Top 5 cities with the highest average CLV, we need to follow the next steps:

  1. First, we aggregate customers by city and calculates the average CLV and number of customers.
clv_location <- fintech_clean %>%
  group_by(city, department, latitude, longitude) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_clv = mean(clv_millions, na.rm = TRUE),
    customers = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(label = city)
  1. We identify Identify the Top 5 cities by average CLV
top_cities <- clv_location %>%
  slice_max(avg_clv, n = 5)
  1. We map it
colombia_map <- map_data("world", region = "Colombia")
map_plot <- ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(
    data = colombia_map,
    aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group),
    fill = "#f7fbff",
    color = "gray80",
    linewidth = 0.3
  ) +
  
  geom_point(
    data = clv_location,
    aes(
      x = longitude,
      y = latitude,
      color = avg_clv,
      size = customers,
      text = paste0(
        "<b>City:</b> ", city,
        "<br><b>Department:</b> ", department,
        "<br><b>Average CLV:</b> ", comma(round(avg_clv, 2)),
        "<br><b>Customers:</b> ", customers
      )
    ),
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +
  
  geom_text(
    data = top_cities,
    aes(x = longitude, y = latitude, label = label),
    size = 3,
    fontface = "bold",
    color = "#08306b",
    vjust = -0.8
  ) +
  
  scale_color_gradient(
    low = "#9ecae1",
    high = "#08306b",
    labels = comma
  ) +
  
  scale_size_continuous(
    range = c(2, 8)
  ) +
  
  coord_quickmap(
    xlim = c(-80, -67),
    ylim = c(-5, 13)
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Average Customer Lifetime Value by Location",
    subtitle = "Bubble size represents number of customers; labels show top 5 cities by average CLV",
    x = "Longitude",
    y = "Latitude",
    color = "Average CLV\n(Millions)",
    size = "Number of\nCustomers"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40"),
    legend.position = "right",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank()
  )
Warning in geom_point(data = clv_location, aes(x = longitude, y = latitude, :
Ignoring unknown aesthetics: text
ggplotly(map_plot, tooltip = "text")

The results reveal that the largest cities in Colombia (Bogotá and Medellín), are not necessarily the locations with the highest average CLV. Instead, several smaller cities exhibit higher value customer concentrations.

This finding suggests that growth opportunities may exist outside major metropolitan areas, indicating that focusing marketing, retention, and expansion strategies on smaller cities could represent an effective approach for increasing profitability.

These geographic patterns also provide useful context for enhancing the predictive CLV model by considering demographic factors as potential predictors of customer value.

4. Predictive Modeling

Following the exploratory analysis, predictive modelling techniques were applied to estimate Customer Lifetime Value. Two predictive approaches were tested to evaluate their performance and robustness. A traditional Linear Regression model and a Random Forest model.

4.1. Linear Regression

Independent variables were selected based on their relevance to customer engagement and financial activity, including transaction frequency, number of active products, average transaction value, customer satisfaction score, and Net Promoter Score (NPS).

clv_model <- lm(
  clv_millions ~
    transaction_frequency +
    active_products +
    average_transaction_value +
    satisfaction_score +
    nps_score,
  data = fintech_clean
)

summary(clv_model)

Call:
lm(formula = clv_millions ~ transaction_frequency + active_products + 
    average_transaction_value + satisfaction_score + nps_score, 
    data = fintech_clean)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
 -457.3  -266.6  -189.4   -25.9 17452.2 

Coefficients:
                            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)                3.385e+01  8.285e+01   0.409    0.683    
transaction_frequency     -1.758e+00  2.104e+00  -0.835    0.404    
active_products            7.926e-01  3.043e+00   0.260    0.795    
average_transaction_value -3.189e-07  9.056e-07  -0.352    0.725    
satisfaction_score         7.189e+01  1.551e+01   4.634  3.6e-06 ***
nps_score                  1.909e-01  7.144e-01   0.267    0.789    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 791.4 on 48717 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.003074,  Adjusted R-squared:  0.002971 
F-statistic: 30.04 on 5 and 48717 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

Once the variables were defined, the model was fitted using the cleaned dataset to quantify the relationship between engagement indicators and CLV.

The model served as a baseline predictive method, allowing for the interpretation of coefficient estimates and the assessment of how individual predictors contribute to customer value.

# Generate predictions
fintech_clean$predicted_clv <- predict(clv_model)

# Define zoom limits (95th percentile)
x_limit <- quantile(fintech_clean$predicted_clv, 0.95)
y_limit <- quantile(fintech_clean$clv_millions, 0.95)

ggplot(fintech_clean,
       aes(x = predicted_clv,
           y = clv_millions,
           color = clv_millions)) +
  
  geom_point(
    alpha = 0.4,
    size = 2
  ) +
  
  # Perfect prediction reference line
  geom_abline(
    slope = 1,
    intercept = 0,
    color = "#08306b",
    linewidth = 1,
    linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  
  scale_color_gradient(
    low = "#9ecae1",
    high = "#08306b",
    labels = comma
  ) +
  
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = comma
  ) +
  
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = comma
  ) +
  
  coord_cartesian(
    xlim = c(0, x_limit),
    ylim = c(0, y_limit)
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs Actual Customer Lifetime Value",
    subtitle = "Dashed line represents perfect prediction",
    x = "Predicted CLV (Millions)",
    y = "Actual CLV (Millions)",
    color = "Actual CLV\n(Millions)"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40"),
    legend.position = "right",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

From the visualization we can see that this model is producing only a few repeated predicted values, while the actual CLV varies a lot.

Several of those predictors are likely discrete or heavily rounded:

  • active_products is a small integer

  • transaction_frequency may take only a limited set of values

  • satisfaction_score and nps_score are rounded scales

The Linear Regresion model combines these values, so many rows end up with the same or very similar prediction.

To address this limitation, a Random Forest model was developed to better capture non-linear relationships and interactions among variables, ultimately improving the accuracy and robustness of CLV predictions.

4.2. Random Forest

The model incorporates key behavioral, financial, and demographic variables. These variables represent important indicators of customer engagement and financial contribution.

rf_model <- randomForest(
  clv_millions ~
    transaction_frequency +
    active_products +
    average_transaction_value +
    satisfaction_score +
    nps_score +
    customer_segment +
    age_bucket,
  data = fintech_clean,
  ntree = 100
)

For this model, categorical variables were converted into factor variables to ensure they were properly handled during model training.

rf_data <- fintech_clean %>%
  select(
    clv_millions,
    transaction_frequency,
    active_products,
    average_transaction_value,
    satisfaction_score,
    nps_score,
    customer_segment,
    age_bucket,
    income_bracket,
    gender,
    occupation
  ) %>%
  na.omit()
rf_data <- rf_data %>%
  mutate(
    customer_segment = as.factor(customer_segment),
    age_bucket = as.factor(age_bucket),
    income_bracket = as.factor(income_bracket),
    gender = as.factor(gender),
    occupation = as.factor(occupation)
  )

The dataset was then divided into training and testing subsets to enable proper evaluation of the model’s predictive performance.

set.seed(123)

train_index <- sample(1:nrow(rf_data), 0.7 * nrow(rf_data))

train_data <- rf_data[train_index, ]
test_data  <- rf_data[-train_index, ]

The model was fitted using the training dataset and the selected behavioral and demographic predictor variables.

set.seed(123)

rf_model <- randomForest(
  clv_millions ~
    transaction_frequency +
    active_products +
    average_transaction_value +
    satisfaction_score +
    nps_score +
    customer_segment +
    age_bucket +
    income_bracket +
    gender +
    occupation,
  data = train_data,
  ntree = 300,
  importance = TRUE
)

The results of the Random Forest model were first examined to evaluate its structure and overall performance characteristics.

rf_summary <- data.frame(
  Metric = c(
    "Number of Trees",
    "Number of Variables Tried at Each Split (mtry)",
    "Mean of Squared Residuals",
    "Percentage Variance Explained"
  ),
  Value = c(
    rf_model$ntree,
    rf_model$mtry,
    round(rf_model$mse[rf_model$ntree], 4),
    round(rf_model$rsq[rf_model$ntree] * 100, 2)
  )
)

knitr::kable(
  rf_summary,
  caption = "Random Forest Model Summary"
)
Random Forest Model Summary
Metric Value
Number of Trees 300.00
Number of Variables Tried at Each Split (mtry) 3.00
Mean of Squared Residuals 555729.64
Percentage Variance Explained 9.54

To better understand the contribution of each predictor to the Random Forest model, a variable importance analysis was conducted.

# Extract importance values from the fitted model
importance_matrix <- randomForest::importance(rf_model)

# Select available importance metric
importance_values <- if ("%IncMSE" %in% colnames(importance_matrix)) {
  importance_matrix[, "%IncMSE"]
} else if ("IncNodePurity" %in% colnames(importance_matrix)) {
  importance_matrix[, "IncNodePurity"]
} else {
  importance_matrix[, 1]
}

# Create dataframe
importance_df <- data.frame(
  Variable = rownames(importance_matrix),
  Importance = importance_values
)

# Convert to relative importance (%)
importance_df <- importance_df %>%
  mutate(
    Importance_Scaled = Importance / max(Importance)
  ) %>%
  arrange(Importance_Scaled) %>%
  mutate(
    Variable = factor(Variable, levels = Variable)
  )

# Plot variable importance
ggplot(
  importance_df,
  aes(
    x = Variable,
    y = Importance_Scaled,
    fill = Importance_Scaled
  )
) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low = "#c6dbef",
    high = "#08306b",
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format()
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Variable Importance in Random Forest Model",
    subtitle = "Predictors ranked by relative contribution to CLV prediction",
    x = "Predictor Variables",
    y = "Relative Importance (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

Although “customer_segment” appears as the most important predictor in the Random Forest model, this result is expected because the variable already captures broad differences in customer engagement and loyalty. For this reason, the more analytically meaningful findings are the strong contributions of “satisfaction_score” and “nps_score”, which emerge as the next most influential predictors of CLV.

Having identified the key drivers of customer value, the next step involved applying the trained model to generate predictions and evaluate its performance on unseen data.

Predicted CLV values were generated using the trained Random Forest model and applied to the testing dataset.

test_data$predicted_clv <- predict(rf_model, newdata = test_data)

A scatter plot comparing predicted and actual CLV values was created to visually assess the performance of the Random Forest model.

x_limit <- quantile(test_data$predicted_clv, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE)
y_limit <- quantile(test_data$clv_millions, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE)

rf_plot <- ggplot(
  test_data,
  aes(
    x = predicted_clv,
    y = clv_millions,
    color = clv_millions,
    text = paste0(
      "Predicted CLV: ", comma(round(predicted_clv, 2)),
      "<br>Actual CLV: ", comma(round(clv_millions, 2))
    )
  )
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 2) +
  
  geom_abline(
    slope = 1,
    intercept = 0,
    color = "#08306b",
    linewidth = 1,
    linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  
  scale_color_gradient(
    low = "#9ecae1",
    high = "#08306b",
    labels = comma
  ) +
  
  coord_cartesian(
    xlim = c(0, x_limit),
    ylim = c(0, y_limit)
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Random Forest: Predicted vs Actual Customer Lifetime Value",
    subtitle = "Dashed line represents perfect prediction",
    x = "Predicted CLV (Millions)",
    y = "Actual CLV (Millions)",
    color = "Actual CLV\n(Millions)"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

ggplotly(rf_plot, tooltip = "text")

Compared to the linear regression model, the Random Forest model produces a more continuous spread of predicted values and captures more variability in CLV.

This improvement occurs because Random Forest models can handle nonlinear relationships and interactions between variables, which are common in customer behavior data.

As a result, the predictions better reflect the complexity of customer engagement patterns and provide a more flexible and robust modelling approach than traditional linear regression.

5. Conclusion

The Retention Risk & Customer Value module provides valuable insights into the factors that influence Customer Lifetime Value and profitability within a fintech platform. Through exploratory modelling and predictive analysis, this module demonstrates how customer engagement behaviors, demographic characteristics, and transactional activity contribute to variations in customer value. While churn-related indicators were explored to understand potential retention risks, the primary focus was the development and evaluation of predictive models capable of estimating future customer value.

The implementation of both Linear Regression and Random Forest models highlighted the advantages of using more flexible machine learning techniques to improve predictive accuracy. Enhanced predictive capabilities enable organizations to anticipate customer value more effectively, allowing businesses to allocate resources more strategically. These improvements support more informed decisions across marketing, sales, and customer retention initiatives.

Overall, the module demonstrates how predictive modelling can transform exploratory insights into actionable forecasts.

The proposed Shiny application will provide an interactive environment for analyzing customer value drivers and evaluating model performance, offering fintech organizations a practical tool to optimize engagement strategies, prioritize high-value segments, and maximize business profitability.
